projects.title: Run a Private AI Journal on Your Laptop
projects.tagline: Use a free open-weight model locally — no cloud, no subscription, total privacy.
projects.inspiredBy: Zyphra releases ZAYA1-8B fully open under Apache 2.0, trained on AMD hardware
projects.outcome: A local AI assistant you can talk to privately, with a saved prompt template for daily journaling.
stretchGoals: Add a second model and compare outputs
Write a shell alias that opens the journal in one command

lessons.title: Why run AI locally?
lessons.content:
Cloud AI services are powerful, but they send every prompt to a remote server. For personal use — journaling, brainstorming, drafting — you might not want that.

Open-weight models like ZAYA1-8B (this week's news) run entirely on your machine. No account, no API key, no billing. The trade-off: they're smaller and less capable than cloud frontier models. But for structured tasks with good prompts, they're surprisingly useful.

**Why this matters for a startup founder:** understanding the cost-capability spectrum of AI models is the single most valuable intuition you can build. Every feature you design for Sublime will sit somewhere on this spectrum.
lessons.checkpoint: You can explain in one sentence why someone would choose a local model over a cloud API.

lessons.title: Install Ollama and pull a model
lessons.content:
Ollama is the simplest way to run open models locally. It handles downloading, quantization, and serving — you just type `ollama run`.

**Install Ollama** (one command, no account needed):
- macOS/Linux: `curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh`
- Windows: download from ollama.com

Then pull a small, fast model:

```bash
ollama pull llama3.2:3b
```

This downloads a ~2GB model. It runs well even on a laptop without a GPU. Once it finishes, test it:

```bash
ollama run llama3.2:3b "What is the capital of Japan?"
```

You should get an answer in a few seconds. That response was generated entirely on your machine — nothing left your laptop.
lessons.code:
# Install
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Pull a small model (~2GB)
ollama pull llama3.2:3b

# Test it
ollama run llama3.2:3b "What is the capital of Japan?"
lessons.checkpoint: Running `ollama list` shows llama3.2:3b and `ollama run llama3.2:3b` answers a question.

lessons.title: Design a system prompt for journaling
lessons.content:
A system prompt shapes how the model behaves for every message in a conversation. For journaling, we want the model to:

1. Ask reflective questions instead of giving generic advice
2. Keep responses short (journaling should be *your* writing)
3. Summarize patterns it notices across the session

Here's a starting prompt — save it to a file called `journal-prompt.txt`:

The key insight: **a well-designed prompt turns a generic model into a specialized tool.** This is the same skill you'll use to build AI features in Sublime — the model is the engine, but the prompt is the product.
lessons.code:
You are a reflective journaling partner. Rules:
- Ask ONE thoughtful follow-up question after each entry.
- Never give advice unless explicitly asked.
- Keep responses under 3 sentences.
- At the end of a session, when I say "summarize", list the 3 main themes from today's entries.

Start by asking what's on my mind today.
lessons.checkpoint: You have a file `journal-prompt.txt` with a system prompt you've customized to your style.

lessons.title: Run a journal session with Modelfile
lessons.content:
Ollama uses `Modelfile` to bundle a system prompt with a model so you can launch it with one command. Create a file called `Modelfile` (no extension):

```
FROM llama3.2:3b
SYSTEM """<paste your journal-prompt.txt content here>"""
PARAMETER temperature 0.7
```

Then create your custom model:

```bash
ollama create my-journal -f Modelfile
ollama run my-journal
```

You're now in a live journal session. Write freely. When you're done, type `summarize` and the model will pull out your recurring themes.

**Experiment:** try changing `temperature` to 0.3 (more focused, predictable) or 0.9 (more creative, surprising) and notice the difference.
lessons.code:
# Create the Modelfile
cat > Modelfile << 'EOF'
FROM llama3.2:3b
SYSTEM """You are a reflective journaling partner. Rules:
- Ask ONE thoughtful follow-up question after each entry.
- Never give advice unless explicitly asked.
- Keep responses under 3 sentences.
- When I say 'summarize', list the 3 main themes from today's entries.
Start by asking what's on my mind today."""
PARAMETER temperature 0.7
EOF

# Build and run
ollama create my-journal -f Modelfile
ollama run my-journal
lessons.checkpoint: Running `ollama run my-journal` starts a journaling conversation that asks you a reflective question.

lessons.title: One-command launcher and daily alias
lessons.content:
The difference between a tool you built and a tool you use is friction. Let's make it zero-friction.

Add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
alias journal='ollama run my-journal'
```

Reload your shell (`source ~/.zshrc`) and now typing `journal` drops you straight into a private AI journaling session.

**Optional:** pipe today's date as the first message so the model knows context:

```bash
alias journal='echo "Today is $(date +"%A, %B %d %Y"). Let\'s begin." | ollama run my-journal'
```
lessons.code:
# Add to ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc
alias journal='ollama run my-journal'

# Reload
source ~/.zshrc

# Now just type:
journal
lessons.checkpoint: Typing `journal` in a new terminal window starts your AI journaling session.

lessons.title: Connecting back: what this means for Sublime
lessons.content:
You just built a personalized AI tool in under two hours with zero cost and zero accounts. Here's what that teaches you about building Sublime:

1. **Prompt = Product.** The model was generic; your system prompt made it a journaling tool. Every AI feature in Sublime is really a prompt design problem.

2. **Local-first is viable.** For features where privacy matters or margins are tight, an open model running on the user's hardware (or a cheap server) can replace API calls entirely.

3. **The cost spectrum.** You now have hands-on intuition for where local models sit vs. cloud APIs — small tasks with good prompts work great locally; complex reasoning still needs frontier models. Designing features around this trade-off is a real competitive advantage.

**Next step:** think about one feature in Sublime where a local/open model would be good enough. Write down what the system prompt would look like.
lessons.checkpoint: You've written down one Sublime feature idea that could run on a local open model, with a draft system prompt.

projects.title: Build a Personal Prompt Library
projects.tagline: Steal California's playbook: systematize AI prompts for your most repetitive tasks.
projects.inspiredBy: California partners with Anthropic to give Claude to every state agency
projects.outcome: A structured markdown file of reusable, tested prompts for your top 5 recurring tasks.
stretchGoals: Add a 'prompt score' for each (rate how well it works 1-5 after each use)
Create a Claude Project with your top prompts as instructions

lessons.title: The California lesson: standardize before you scale
lessons.content:
This week, California announced a state-wide partnership with Anthropic — giving Claude to every agency, city and county. But the real story isn't the tool, it's the method: they're pairing it with **standardized training and prompt templates** so thousands of workers get consistent results.

You're one person, but the same principle applies. Most of us use AI ad-hoc: open a chat, type something vaguely, get a vaguely useful answer. A prompt library flips that — you invest 5 minutes writing a great prompt once, then reuse it every time.

**The ROI math:** if a prompt saves you 10 minutes on a task you do 3x per week, that's 26 hours per year from one prompt.
lessons.checkpoint: You understand the difference between ad-hoc prompting and a reusable prompt library.

lessons.title: Audit your week for repeat tasks
lessons.content:
Open a blank note and spend 10 minutes listing tasks from your last week that:

- You've done more than twice
- Involve writing, summarizing, analyzing, or formatting
- Feel tedious but don't require deep creative thought

Examples:
- Summarizing meeting notes into action items
- Writing follow-up emails after calls
- Turning rough ideas into structured outlines
- Reviewing a document for tone and clarity
- Drafting social media posts from a longer piece

Pick your **top 5**. These are the tasks worth building prompts for. Rank them by frequency × tedium.
lessons.checkpoint: You have a ranked list of 5 repetitive tasks that AI could help with.

lessons.title: The anatomy of a reusable prompt
lessons.content:
A reusable prompt has four parts:

1. **Role** — who is the AI acting as? ("You are a concise technical editor.")
2. **Task** — what specifically to do ("Rewrite the following for clarity...")
3. **Constraints** — rules and format ("Max 3 bullet points. No jargon.")
4. **Placeholder** — where your input goes ("Text to edit: {{INPUT}}")

Here's a real example for summarizing meeting notes:

```
You are a project manager's assistant.

Summarize these meeting notes into:
1. Three key decisions made
2. Action items with owners (format: "- [ ] Task — @Owner")
3. One unresolved question to follow up on

Keep the total under 150 words. Use plain language.

Meeting notes:
{{PASTE NOTES HERE}}
```

Notice: no fluff, very specific output format, clear constraints. **Test it** — paste real notes in and see if the output matches what you'd write by hand. Tweak until it does.
lessons.code:
# Template: Meeting Notes → Action Items

You are a project manager's assistant.

Summarize these meeting notes into:
1. Three key decisions made
2. Action items with owners (format: "- [ ] Task — @Owner")
3. One unresolved question to follow up on

Keep the total under 150 words. Use plain language.

Meeting notes:
{{PASTE NOTES HERE}}
lessons.checkpoint: You've written and tested one reusable prompt that produces consistent results.

lessons.title: Build prompts for all five tasks
lessons.content:
Now create a markdown file called `prompts.md` and write one prompt section for each of your 5 tasks. Use this structure for each:

```markdown
## Task: [Name]
**When to use:** [one line]
**Time saved:** ~[X] min per use

### Prompt
[Your prompt with {{PLACEHOLDER}} for input]

### Example output
[Paste one real result so future-you remembers what good looks like]

---
```

**Tips:**
- Be embarrassingly specific in constraints. "Write casually" → "Write like a smart friend texting, not a LinkedIn post."
- Include output format: bullet points, numbered list, table, JSON — the model follows formatting instructions well.
- Test each prompt with real input before saving it. Adjust until the output is copy-paste-ready at least 80% of the time.
lessons.checkpoint: You have a `prompts.md` file with 5 tested, reusable prompt sections.

lessons.title: Make it frictionless to access
lessons.content:
A prompt library only works if it's faster to open than to retype from memory. Options:

- **Simplest:** Pin `prompts.md` in your file manager or bookmark it in your notes app.
- **Better:** Create a Claude Project and paste your top prompts into the Project Instructions. Every new conversation in that project automatically uses them.
- **Power move:** Use a text expander (like Raycast snippets on Mac, or Espanso on any OS — both free). Type `;meeting` and it expands to your full meeting-notes prompt.

Pick one method and set it up now. The goal: going from "I need to summarize these notes" to having the prompt ready should take under 5 seconds.
lessons.checkpoint: You can access any of your 5 prompts in under 5 seconds using your chosen method.

lessons.title: Connecting back: prompts as a product skill
lessons.content:
You just built what California is spending millions to roll out: a structured prompt library that turns AI from a novelty into a reliable tool.

For Sublime, this skill translates directly:

1. **Every AI feature is a prompt.** When you build AI into Sublime, the prompts you write for users ARE the product experience. You just practiced the craft.

2. **Prompt templates = product templates.** If Sublime helps users with repetitive tasks, shipping pre-built prompt templates (like your `prompts.md`) is a feature users will pay for.

3. **Iteration matters.** You noticed that first-draft prompts rarely work perfectly. Build that iteration loop into Sublime's product process — user feedback on AI output quality is gold.

**Next step:** look at your 5 prompts and ask: could any of these be a feature inside Sublime?
lessons.checkpoint: You've identified at least one prompt that could become a Sublime feature.

projects.title: Build an AI Model Comparison Scorecard
projects.tagline: Sonnet 5, GPT-5.6, open models — test them yourself instead of trusting benchmarks.
projects.inspiredBy: Anthropic launches Claude Sonnet 5 — now the default for every Free and Pro user
projects.outcome: A Python script and spreadsheet that benchmarks 3+ models on tasks relevant to YOUR work, not generic leaderboards.
stretchGoals: Add automated scoring with a judge model
Publish your scorecard as a blog post

modules.title: Module 1: Why Generic Benchmarks Lie to You

lessons.title: Leaderboards vs. your actual tasks
lessons.content:
This week, Sonnet 5 launched claiming to be "close to Opus 4.8 on many tasks" and GPT-5.5 Instant reported a 52.5% hallucination reduction. Those are aggregate numbers across thousands of test cases.

The problem: **your tasks aren't average.** A model that ranks #1 on coding benchmarks might be mediocre at the specific kind of email drafting you do daily. The only benchmark that matters is one built from your real work.

In this project, you'll build a personal evaluation harness — a set of test prompts drawn from your actual tasks, with criteria you define, scored across multiple models. It takes ~2 hours but gives you decision-making power that no leaderboard post can.

**This is a startup founder superpower:** knowing which model to use for which task, based on evidence rather than hype.
lessons.checkpoint: You can explain why a model that tops a public leaderboard might underperform on your specific use case.

lessons.title: Design your test suite
lessons.content:
Create a file called `eval_prompts.json`. It holds 5-8 test cases — real prompts from your work, each with criteria for what a good answer looks like.

Structure:

```json
[
  {
    "id": "summarize-email",
    "prompt": "Summarize this email thread into 3 bullet points...\n\n[paste a real email]",
    "criteria": "Captures all action items; under 80 words; no hallucinated details",
    "category": "summarization"
  }
]
```

Good test cases to include:
- A summarization task (email, article, meeting notes)
- A writing task (draft a message in your voice)
- A reasoning task (analyze a tradeoff, debug a problem)
- A formatting task (turn messy text into structured output)
- A knowledge task (something you can fact-check)

Use **real data from your work** — that's the whole point.
lessons.checkpoint: You have `eval_prompts.json` with at least 5 test cases using real data from your work.

lessons.title: Build the eval runner script
lessons.content:
We'll write a Python script that sends each test prompt to multiple models and saves the results. It uses free tiers only:

- **Claude Sonnet 5** via claude.ai (free tier — copy-paste)
- **ChatGPT** via chatgpt.com (free tier — copy-paste)
- **Local model** via Ollama (if you did Project 1)

For the local model, we can automate it. For cloud models on free tiers, the script generates the prompts and you paste the responses back. The script then collates everything into a scorecard.

Create `eval_runner.py`:

The script reads your test cases, optionally hits a local Ollama model, and writes a `results.json` file with all responses organized for scoring.
lessons.checkpoint: Running `python eval_runner.py` produces a `results.json` with local model responses filled in.

lessons.title: Score and compare
lessons.content:
Open `results.json` and paste the cloud model responses (run each prompt in claude.ai and chatgpt.com free tier). Then score each response 1-5 on the criteria you defined.

Add a `scores` field to each entry:

```json
"scores": {
  "llama3.2:3b": {"score": 3, "notes": "Got the gist but missed one action item"},
  "sonnet-5": {"score": 5, "notes": "Perfect, nailed the format"},
  "gpt-free": {"score": 4, "notes": "Good but slightly verbose"}
}
```

Then generate your scorecard — a simple markdown table:

| Task | Local (3B) | Sonnet 5 | GPT Free |
|------|-----------|----------|----------|
| Summarize email | 3 | 5 | 4 |
| Draft followup | 2 | 5 | 4 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| **Average** | **2.6** | **4.8** | **4.0** |

You now have data, not opinions. You know exactly which model to use for which task in YOUR workflow.
lessons.checkpoint: You have a completed scorecard table showing how 3+ models perform on your specific tasks.

lessons.title: Read the scorecard like a founder
lessons.content:
Look at your scorecard and answer these questions:

1. **Is there a clear winner?** Often there isn't — different models win on different task types. That's the insight.

2. **Where does the local model surprise you?** If it scores 4+ on any task, that's a feature you could run at near-zero cost.

3. **What's the quality gap between free and paid tiers?** This tells you your "cost of quality" — how much it's worth to use a paid API vs. free.

4. **Which tasks have the widest score spread?** Those are where model selection matters most — and where Sublime could differentiate by routing to the right model.

Write down your top 3 findings. These are now data-backed beliefs about the AI landscape — much more valuable than reading someone else's benchmark blog.
lessons.checkpoint: You've written 3 data-backed findings about model strengths/weaknesses for your specific use cases.

lessons.title: Connecting back: model routing as a Sublime feature
lessons.content:
You just built what AI companies charge enterprise customers thousands for: a custom evaluation framework.

For Sublime:

1. **Model routing is a product feature.** Your scorecard showed that no single model wins everything. A product that automatically routes tasks to the best model (cheap local model for simple tasks, frontier model for complex ones) saves money and improves quality.

2. **Evaluation is ongoing.** New models drop every few weeks (Sonnet 5 and GPT-5.6 both landed this week). Your eval script lets you benchmark a new model in 30 minutes instead of guessing.

3. **Your test suite is your moat.** The prompts in `eval_prompts.json` encode YOUR domain knowledge. A competitor using generic benchmarks will make worse model choices than you.

**Next step:** rerun this evaluation whenever a major model launches. Your script makes it a 30-minute task instead of a day-long exercise.
lessons.checkpoint: You've identified whether model routing could be a feature in Sublime and which tasks would benefit most.

projects.title: Build a Tiny AI Agent That Searches and Summarizes
projects.tagline: Code-first agents in ~50 lines — no framework bloat, just Python.
projects.inspiredBy: Code-first agents go mainstream: Hugging Face's smolagents fits in ~1,000 lines
projects.outcome: A working agent that takes a question, searches the web, reads the results, and writes a concise answer — all in under 100 lines of Python.
stretchGoals: Add a file-writing tool so the agent saves reports to disk
Give the agent a calculator tool for numerical questions

lessons.title: Demystifying 'AI agents'
lessons.content:
"AI agents" is 2026's most overhyped buzzword, but the core idea is simple:

**An agent is a loop:** the model receives a task → decides which tool to use → reads the tool's output → decides the next step → repeats until done.

That's it. The "intelligence" is the model's ability to pick the right tool and interpret results. The "agent framework" is just the loop that runs this.

This week's news: Hugging Face's `smolagents` proved you don't need a 10,000-line framework — the core routing logic fits in ~1,000 lines. We'll go even simpler: our agent will be under 100 lines, using only Python's standard library plus one model call.

**Why this matters:** if you understand the loop, you can build agents without depending on any framework. Frameworks change; the pattern doesn't.
lessons.checkpoint: You can describe the agent loop (task → tool selection → execution → observation → repeat) in your own words.

lessons.title: Set up the project
lessons.content:
Create a new directory and set up a minimal Python project:

```bash
mkdir tiny-agent && cd tiny-agent
```

We need:
- Python 3.10+ (pre-installed on most systems)
- An Ollama model running locally (from Project 1), OR access to a free AI chat

If you have Ollama running:
```bash
ollama pull llama3.2:3b
```

We'll build three files:
1. `tools.py` — the tools the agent can use (web search, page reader)
2. `agent.py` — the agent loop
3. `run.py` — the entry point

No pip installs needed — we're using only the standard library.
lessons.code:
mkdir tiny-agent && cd tiny-agent
touch tools.py agent.py run.py
lessons.checkpoint: You have a `tiny-agent` directory with three empty Python files.

modules.title: Module 2: Tools and the Agent Loop

lessons.title: Build the tools
lessons.content:
An agent's tools are just Python functions with descriptions the model can read. We'll build two:

1. **`web_search(query)`** — uses DuckDuckGo's HTML (no API key needed) to get search snippets
2. **`read_page(url)`** — fetches a URL and extracts the text

Each tool returns a string the model will read as its "observation." Keep outputs concise — models work better with focused input.

The key design rule: **tools should be pure functions with clear input/output.** The model picks which tool to call and what arguments to pass; the tool just does the work and returns text.
lessons.code: # tools.py import re import urllib.request import urllib.parse def web_search(query): """Search the web and return top 5 result snippets.""" url = "https://html.duckduckgo.com/html/?q=" + urllib.parse.quote(query) req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={"User-Agent": "TinyAgent/1.0"}) with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=15) as resp: html = resp.read().decode("utf-8", "replace") results = re.findall( r'class="result__a"[^>]*href="(.*?)".*?class="result__snippet">(.*?)', html, re.DOTALL ) lines = [] for url_encoded, snippet in results[:5]: text = re.sub(r'<.*?>', '', snippet).strip() lines.append(f"- {text}") return "\n".join(lines) if lines else "No results found." def read_page(url): """Fetch a URL and return first 1500 chars of text.""" req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={"User-Agent": "TinyAgent/1.0"}) with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=15) as resp: html = resp.read().decode("utf-8", "replace") text = re.sub(r'', '', html, flags=re.DOTALL) text = re.sub(r'', '', text, flags=re.DOTALL) text = re.sub(r'<.*?>', ' ', text) text = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text).strip() return text[:1500] TOOLS = { "web_search": { "fn": web_search, "desc": "Search the web. Input: a search query string." }, "read_page": { "fn": read_page, "desc": "Read a web page. Input: a URL." } }
lessons.checkpoint: Running `python -c "from tools import web_search; print(web_search('python programming'))"` returns search snippets.

lessons.title: Build the agent loop
lessons.content:
The agent loop has four steps:

1. **System prompt** tells the model it has tools and how to call them
2. **User message** with the question
3. **Parse** the model's response for tool calls (we use a simple `ACTION: tool_name(input)` format)
4. **Execute** the tool, append the result as an observation, and loop back

The loop stops when the model outputs `FINAL ANSWER:` instead of an action.

This is the entire agent — no magic, no framework. The model's reasoning ability drives the loop; our code just connects the pieces.
lessons.checkpoint: You can read through `agent.py` and explain what happens at each step of the loop.

modules.title: Module 3: Run It and Learn From It

lessons.title: Test your agent
lessons.content:
Create the entry point and run it:

```python
# run.py
from agent import run_agent
import sys

question = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or "What is the latest AI news today?"
print(f"\nQuestion: {question}\n")
answer = run_agent(question)
print(f"\n=== ANSWER ===\n{answer}")
```

Run it:
```bash
python run.py "What did Anthropic announce this week?"
```

Watch the steps print out — you'll see the model decide to search, read the results, and formulate an answer. It won't be perfect (a 3B model has limits), but it works.

**Experiment:** Try questions that need 2+ tool calls, like "Compare the price of Claude Sonnet 5 vs GPT-5.6." Watch how the agent chains searches.
lessons.code:
# run.py
from agent import run_agent
import sys

question = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or "What is the latest AI news today?"
print(f"\nQuestion: {question}\n")
answer = run_agent(question)
print(f"\n=== ANSWER ===\n{answer}")
lessons.checkpoint: Running `python run.py 'your question'` produces a multi-step agent trace ending in a coherent answer.

lessons.title: Connecting back: agents as Sublime features
lessons.content:
You just built a working AI agent in under 100 lines. Here's what this means for Sublime:

1. **Agents are just loops + tools.** You don't need LangChain, CrewAI, or any framework to ship agentic features. A founder who understands the pattern can build exactly what's needed.

2. **The tools are the product.** The model's reasoning ability is rented (via API or local model). What makes YOUR agent valuable is the specific tools you give it. For Sublime, the tools would connect to your users' data and workflows.

3. **Small models can drive simple agents.** A 3B model successfully picked tools and chained actions. For Sublime features with well-defined tool sets, you don't always need a frontier model — which means lower cost per user.

4. **The failure modes are instructive.** Where did your agent stumble? Those failure modes (wrong tool choice, bad parsing, hallucinated actions) are the same ones you'll need to handle in production. You've now seen them firsthand.

**Next step:** think about one Sublime workflow that could be an agent — what tools would it need?
lessons.checkpoint: You've listed one potential Sublime workflow as an agent, with the specific tools it would need.

projects.title: Build an AI Funding Tracker in a Spreadsheet
projects.tagline: Turn funding news into a structured dataset you can query and learn from.
projects.inspiredBy: Chamath Palihapitiya goes full-time CEO at 8090 Labs after $135M Series A
projects.outcome: A structured spreadsheet tracking AI startup funding rounds with a reusable AI prompt to fill it from any news article.
stretchGoals: Add a chart showing funding trends over time
Write a Python script to auto-fetch and parse funding news

lessons.title: Funding signals decode the market for free
lessons.content:
This week, Chamath went full-time CEO at 8090 Labs with a $135M Series A. South Korea committed $880B to AI infrastructure. These aren't just headlines — they're data points.

Every funding round tells you:
- **What problem investors believe is worth solving** (the company's pitch)
- **How much conviction there is** (round size, investor tier)
- **Where the market is heading** (sector, geography, timing)

Most founders read funding news passively. You're going to turn it into a structured dataset — a personal market intelligence tool that gets smarter every week.

**Why a spreadsheet?** Because it's free, it's queryable, and the act of structuring data forces you to think about what actually matters. No fancy tools needed.
lessons.checkpoint: You can name 3 things a funding round tells you about market direction.

lessons.title: Design the tracker schema
lessons.content:
Open any spreadsheet (Google Sheets, LibreOffice, Excel — all free). Create these columns:

| Column | Example |
|--------|--------|
| Date | 2026-07-01 |
| Company | 8090 Labs |
| Round | Series A |
| Amount | $135M |
| Lead Investor | Salesforce Ventures |
| Sector | AI-native software |
| Pain Point | Automated business software |
| CEO Background | Chamath Palihapitiya, former Social Capital |
| My Take | Signals that AI-native SaaS is investable, not just AI infra |

The "Pain Point" and "My Take" columns are the most valuable — they force you to think like an investor. **Fill in the first row** with the 8090 Labs data from this week's news.
lessons.code:
Date,Company,Round,Amount,Lead Investor,Sector,Pain Point,CEO Background,My Take
2026-07-01,8090 Labs,Series A,$135M,Salesforce Ventures,AI-native software,Automated business software,Chamath Palihapitiya - former Social Capital,AI-native SaaS is investable beyond infra plays
lessons.checkpoint: You have a spreadsheet with headers and at least one row filled in from real news.

lessons.title: Build a prompt to extract funding data
lessons.content:
Filling the tracker by hand is slow. Let's build an AI prompt that extracts structured data from any funding article.

Here's the prompt template:

```
Extract funding data from this article into CSV format with these exact columns:
Date, Company, Round, Amount, Lead Investor, Sector, Pain Point, CEO Background

Rules:
- Date in YYYY-MM-DD format
- Amount with $ and M/B suffix
- Pain Point: one sentence on what problem they solve
- If any field is unknown, write "unknown"
- Output ONLY the CSV row, no headers, no explanation

Article:
{{PASTE ARTICLE TEXT}}
```

Test it with a real article. Paste the output into your spreadsheet. Add your "My Take" column manually — that's the part the AI can't do for you.

**Pro tip:** save this prompt as a snippet (see the Prompt Library project) so you can use it in seconds whenever you see funding news.
lessons.code:
Extract funding data from this article into CSV format with these exact columns:
Date, Company, Round, Amount, Lead Investor, Sector, Pain Point, CEO Background

Rules:
- Date in YYYY-MM-DD format
- Amount with $ and M/B suffix
- Pain Point: one sentence on what problem they solve
- If any field is unknown, write "unknown"
- Output ONLY the CSV row, no headers, no explanation

Article:
{{PASTE ARTICLE TEXT}}
lessons.checkpoint: Your extraction prompt correctly outputs a CSV row from a real funding article.

lessons.title: Batch-fill from this week's signal
lessons.content:
Go through this week's AI Signal news and find every item that mentions funding, investment, or acquisition. Use your extraction prompt on each one.

From this week's seed data alone, you should find at least:
- 8090 Labs ($135M Series A)
- South Korea's $880B national plan (government investment, not VC — note the difference)

Also search "AI funding this week" and add 3-5 more from outside the Signal. Your tracker should have at least 5 rows when you're done.

Now look at the data as a whole:
- Which sectors got the most funding?
- Are rounds getting bigger or smaller?
- Any geographic patterns?

You're doing what analysts at venture firms do, but free, in 30 minutes, powered by AI for the grunt work.
lessons.checkpoint: Your tracker has 5+ rows and you've noted one pattern you see in the data.

modules.title: Module 3: Making It Useful for Sublime

lessons.title: Weekly update ritual (10 minutes/week)
lessons.content:
A tracker is only useful if you maintain it. Here's a sustainable ritual:

**Every Monday morning, 10 minutes:**
1. Open the AI Signal or your RSS reader
2. Search "AI funding" in Google News
3. Paste any new articles into your extraction prompt
4. Add the CSV rows to your spreadsheet
5. Write one "My Take" sentence for each
6. Glance at the full sheet — any trend jumps out?

That's it. Over a month, you'll have 20-30 data points. Over a quarter, 60-90. That's enough to see real patterns — which sectors are heating up, which are cooling, where the smart money is going.

Set a recurring calendar event now: "AI Funding Tracker — 10 min" every Monday at 9 AM.
lessons.checkpoint: You have a calendar event set for weekly tracker updates.

lessons.title: Connecting back: market intelligence for Sublime
lessons.content:
You just built a personal market intelligence tool from a headline and a spreadsheet.

For Sublime:

1. **Structured data is power.** Turning unstructured news into queryable data is exactly what Sublime could do for users. Your extraction prompt is a prototype of that feature.

2. **AI + human judgment.** The AI did the extraction; you added the insight ("My Take"). The best AI products don't replace human judgment — they clear the path so humans can focus on judgment.

3. **Patterns over headlines.** After a few weeks, your tracker will reveal things no single article shows: sector trends, investor clustering, funding velocity. Sublime could offer this kind of longitudinal insight to its users.

**Next step:** review your tracker and ask: does the data suggest any adjacent market Sublime should watch? Write down one idea.
lessons.checkpoint: You've identified one market insight from your tracker that could inform Sublime's direction.

projects.title: Build a Tiny Chrome Extension That Summarizes Pages
projects.tagline: Ship a real browser extension in under 2 hours — no frameworks, no accounts.
projects.inspiredBy: California partners with Anthropic to give Claude to every state agency
projects.outcome: A working Chrome extension that summarizes the current webpage using a local AI model via Ollama.
stretchGoals: Add a keyboard shortcut to trigger summarization
Store summaries in extension storage for a reading history

lessons.title: How Chrome extensions actually work
lessons.content:
A Chrome extension is just a folder with a `manifest.json` and some HTML/JS files. No special tools, no build step, no account needed to develop locally.

The three key pieces:
- **manifest.json** — metadata and permissions (which sites it can access, what it can do)
- **popup.html/js** — the UI that appears when you click the extension icon
- **content script (optional)** — JS that runs inside web pages

We'll build a popup extension: you click the icon, it grabs the current page's text, sends it to a local Ollama model, and shows the summary.

**Why this project?** California is rolling out AI assistants to every state agency. You're building the same concept — an AI layer on top of existing workflows — but as a browser extension you control.
lessons.checkpoint: You can name the 3 core files in a Chrome extension and what each does.

lessons.title: Create the project scaffold
lessons.content:
Create a folder called `ai-summarizer-ext` with these files:

**manifest.json** tells Chrome what your extension does. We need:
- `activeTab` permission (access the current tab's content)
- A popup (the UI)
- An icon (we'll use an emoji-based SVG for simplicity)

**popup.html** is minimal — a button, a loading indicator, and a results area.

Create all files as shown in the code block. The popup is deliberately simple: one button, one output area. No frameworks.

To load it in Chrome:
1. Go to `chrome://extensions`
2. Enable "Developer mode" (toggle in top-right)
3. Click "Load unpacked" and select your folder
4. You'll see the extension icon appear in your toolbar
lessons.checkpoint: Your extension folder has manifest.json and popup.html, and it loads in Chrome without errors.

lessons.title: Extract page text and call Ollama
lessons.content:
Create `popup.js` — the brain of the extension. It does three things:

1. **Extracts text** from the current tab using Chrome's scripting API
2. **Sends it to Ollama** running locally on port 11434
3. **Displays the summary** in the popup

Ollama exposes a REST API at `http://localhost:11434/api/generate`. We send a POST with the model name, a prompt containing the page text, and `stream: false` to get the full response at once.

The prompt is critical: we ask for a concise summary with key takeaways, not a verbose rewrite. We also truncate the page text to 4000 characters to keep the local model fast.

**Prerequisite:** Make sure Ollama is running (`ollama serve` in a terminal) with a model pulled (`ollama pull llama3.2:3b`).
lessons.checkpoint: Clicking 'Summarize' extracts page text and shows a summary from your local Ollama model.

lessons.title: Handle edge cases and polish
lessons.checkpoint: Your extension gracefully handles Ollama being offline, pages with no text, and works on 3 different page types.

modules.title: Module 3: Ship It and Learn From It

lessons.title: Use it for a day
lessons.content:
Keep the extension installed and use it throughout your day. Every time you open a long article, click Summarize first.

Notice:
- **Where it saves time:** long articles, technical docs, research papers
- **Where it fails:** pages with mostly images, very short pages, content behind paywalls
- **Quality patterns:** does the model miss nuance? Hallucinate details? Get the gist but miss the point?

Write down your observations. This is product research — you're experiencing the UX of an AI tool firsthand, noticing friction points that benchmarks can't measure.

After a day, you'll have informed opinions about:
- Whether local models are "good enough" for summarization
- What UX touches would make this genuinely useful vs. a novelty
- Whether this is worth building into a more polished product
lessons.checkpoint: You've used the extension on 5+ pages and written down what worked, what didn't, and why.

lessons.title: Connecting back: browser extensions as Sublime features
lessons.content:
You just shipped a real browser extension with AI capabilities in under 2 hours. Here's what this means for Sublime:

1. **Browser extensions are underrated distribution.** California is deploying AI as a centralized tool; a browser extension meets users where they already are. If Sublime has a web-facing product, an extension is one of the cheapest distribution channels.

2. **Local AI for privacy-sensitive features.** Your extension sends nothing to the cloud. For any Sublime feature handling sensitive user data, this architecture — local model, no API calls — eliminates both cost and privacy concerns.

3. **The manifest v3 pattern.** Chrome's extension platform is well-documented, free to develop on, and reaches billions of browsers. The barrier is low; the reach is enormous.

4. **Prompt quality matters more than model quality.** A well-crafted summarization prompt on a 3B model often beats a lazy prompt on a frontier model. That's a cost advantage Sublime can exploit.

**Next step:** list one Sublime workflow that could live as a browser extension. What data would it access, and what would the AI do with it?
lessons.checkpoint: You've identified one potential Sublime browser extension and described what it would do.
